case: C.R No. 850-P of 2026 Muhammad Zeeshan Ud Din Vs Mst. Rukhsana Shafique & another
remarks:
Section 35 & 35-A read with Order XLI rule 33 CPC.
• The Code of Civil Procedure draws a clear distinction between ordinary costs under Section 35, C.P.C. and compensatory costs under Section 35-A, C.P.C. While Section 35 confers a discretionary power upon the Court to award ordinary costs of litigation with a view to indemnifying the successful party for expenses reasonably incurred in prosecuting or defending the proceedings, Section 35-A creates a special and penal jurisdiction enabling the Court to award compensatory costs only where the claim or defence is found to be false or vexatious to the knowledge of the party raising it and the statutory conditions prescribed therein have been strictly fulfilled. More importantly, the jurisdiction to award compensatory costs under Section 35-A, C.P.C. is vested exclusively in the Court of first instance trying the suit. The appellate Court does not possess an independent or concurrent jurisdiction to exercise that power. This legal position is placed beyond doubt by the proviso to Order XLI, Rule 33, C.P.C., which expressly restrains an appellate Court from making any order under Section 35-A in respect of an objection upon which the trial Court has omitted or refused to pass such an order. Although Rule 33 confers wide discretionary powers upon an appellate Court to pass any decree or order which ought to have been passed by the Court below, the proviso engrafts a specific statutory limitation by excluding the jurisdiction to award compensatory costs under Section 35-A, C.P.C. Consequently, while an appellate Court may affirm, modify or set aside ordinary costs awarded under Section 35, C.P.C., it is denuded of jurisdiction to independently impose compensatory costs.
• In the present case, the learned appellate Court rightly concluded that the plaint could not have been rejected under Order VII, Rule 11, C.P.C., as the controversy between the parties involved disputed and intricate questions of fact and law requiring determination after framing of issues and recording of evidence. The order of remand, therefore, suffers from no legal infirmity and calls for no interference. However, while allowing the appeal, the learned appellate Court further burdened the petitioner with costs of Rs. 50,000/-. The nature and tenor of the order unmistakably demonstrate that the amount was imposed as compensatory costs and not as ordinary costs under Section 35, C.P.C. Such an exercise of jurisdiction is expressly prohibited by the proviso to Order XLI, Rule 33, C.P.C. and is, therefore, without lawful authority. Accordingly, this civil revision fails on merits insofar as the remand of the suit for trial is concerned, as the learned appellate Court rightly held that the controversy could not be adjudicated within the limited scope of Order VII, Rule 11, C.P.C. and necessarily required a full-fledged trial. However, the direction imposing costs of Rs. 50,000/- being beyond the jurisdiction vested in the appellate Court under Section 35-A read with the proviso to Order XLI, Rule 33, C.P.C., is declared illegal and is hereby set aside.
